storm.summary_layperson: Scattered thunderstorm cells are clustered 30–48 miles to the southwest but are tracking northwestward — nearly parallel to the Sound — not toward the cove, and low atmospheric instability makes them unlikely to intensify or change course.
storm.local_effect_blurb: The marine layer is weak today (Sound–air temperature gap 6°F, below the 10°F activation threshold), so no coastal blocking mechanism is in play — storm track is the key variable.
storm.detail_html:
Radar at 4:22 PM EDT shows a cluster of cells 30–48 miles to the southwest, the nearest at 30 miles bearing SW with 40 dBZ, the strongest producing 52 dBZ at 46 miles WSW. Radar-measured system motion (tracked across two KOKX scans 26 minutes apart) is 10 kt toward NNW — the cluster is drifting roughly northwestward. Because the cells sit to our SW and are moving toward the NNW, their motion is nearly perpendicular to the straight-line path to Greenwich Cove. The arrival engine finds every cell quasi-stationary relative to the cove — none are closing on us.

Unless the system changes direction or new cells develop to our immediate west or northwest, the main cluster should continue to drift past the cove without a direct strike. The scenario to watch is cell regeneration northwest of us — that quadrant lacks the Sound buffer — but as of this fetch there is no radar evidence of that developing.

Atmospheric instability (CAPE) is low at 480 J/kg. The NWS Area Forecast Discussion described conditions as marginal to locally moderate instability, with the severe and flash flood threat centered on the NYC and NJ metro area and explicitly decreasing moving north and east from there — placing the cove in the diminishing-threat zone. Bulk shear at 22 kt is moderate; in this low-CAPE environment that shear is not organizing vigorous new convection.

The coastal protective mechanism is not formally active today: the Sound is 73°F against an inland air temperature of 79°F, a 6°F gap that falls short of the 10°F threshold required. That said, with CAPE so low, any cell that did approach from the SW would still cross the cooler Sound and lose surface fuel — meaning outflow wind would be the more likely experience than sustained rain or thunder. This passive backstop exists, but storm track is what's keeping the cove clear, not an active marine-layer choke.

The Air Quality Alert in effect through 11 PM tonight is unrelated to convection (ozone/particulate). No severe thunderstorm watch or warning is active for the Greenwich area. The key variables to watch over the next few hours: any radar trend showing storm motion shifting toward ENE, or new development northwest of the cove. If neither occurs, risk stays at moderate-but-not-local.

wind.tactics_blurb: SSW at 10–15 kt through the evening with pressure channeling along the west shore — start right, work the right side of the first beat. Expect a steady, non-oscillating direction through sunset, lightening to 5–7 kt by 9–10 PM.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Regime A+ (borderline): inland air is 79°F against Sound water at 73°F, a 6.1°F delta that just clears the 5°F thermal gate. The gradient is an onshore SSW flow at 11–13 kt — classified A+ because an onshore gradient under 15 kt reinforces rather than opposes the sea breeze. One caveat: direction agreement across all stations is 0.93 and the southerly flow is uniform both coastal and inland, a signature of gradient-dominant flow. The thermal component is real but subtle; expect the wind to behave closer to a steady gradient than a classic thermal SB.

RYC WeatherLink is reading 11 kt S (190°) with 16 kt gusts at 4:30 PM. KBDR confirms 13 kt SSW. HRRR projects a brief peak to 15 kt at 5 PM, then a steady easing: 11 kt at 6 PM, 8–9 kt from 7–8 PM, fading to 5–7 kt by 9–10 PM. HRRR and observed western-LIS directions diverge by only 2° — negligible, no correction applied. The forecast direction column is raw HRRR throughout the window.

Sunset is at approximately 8:20 PM. In a pure thermal (Regime A), wind dies 30–60 minutes near or before sunset. The SSW synoptic gradient in Regime A+ means the flow persists 30–60 minutes beyond the thermal collapse — expect meaningful wind (7–9 kt) through 9 PM, lightening to 5–7 kt by 10 PM. HRRR's extended outlook (10 PM onward) shows a 3–6.5 kt range with a mean of 5 kt, consistent with a gradient that drains gradually rather than collapses.

For cove tactics: SSW at 195–213° puts the wind slightly right of the cove's N–S axis. This channels along the west shore (Field Point / Indian Harbor / Belle Haven side), giving the right side of the upwind leg more pressure than the left (Greenwich Point / Tod's Point). Direction is rock-steady — 0.93 ensemble agreement across 27 stations, no significant oscillations expected. This is not a shifty day; gains come from position, not shift timing. Start right if possible and hold the right side on the first beat.

One wildcard: the SW storm cells, while not tracking directly at us, could produce outflow pulses — brief puffs to 15–20 kt followed by a lull — if they drift closer. Watch RYC for a sudden pressure drop or gust spike. Given the NNW storm track, this is a background risk rather than the primary scenario as of 4:30 PM.

data_caveats: Buoy 44040 stale (last obs 120 min ago — over-water wind and SST unavailable)
Wind regime A+ is borderline — deltaT 6.1°F is at the thermal gate floor; synoptic gradient likely dominant
